glossary.definition: sets of assumptions in the field of artificial intelligence that model intellectual behaviour.
glossary.sentence: There are two main paradigms of thinking or sets of assumptions in the field of artificial intelligence: symbolic artificial intelligence and connectionist artificial intelligence.

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence Symbolic artificial intelligence An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons) An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons)
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence Symbolic artificial intelligence An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons) An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems

glossary.definition: a hypothesis defined by Newell and Simon in 1976 that states a physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action.
glossary.sentence: It defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved and is formulated as follows: “A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action” (Newell and Simon, 1976).

glossary.definition: meaningful physical patterns that can represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality.
glossary.sentence: A physical symbol system includes (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009; Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality.

glossary.definition: symbols that are connected to each other in some way, depicting important aspects of a problem domain.
glossary.sentence: symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way. Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain; operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures;

glossary.definition: actions that allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures, such as constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying.
glossary.sentence: operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.

glossary.definition: transforming physical symbols according to rules.
glossary.sentence: Summarising all of the above, it can be said that " all intelligent behavior essentially involves transforming physical symbols according to rules "

glossary.definition: generalisations available in the relevant problem domain that are explicitly represented through a set of symbols.
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems based on this paradigm therefore explicitly represent problem domain knowledge (objects and their relations) through a set of symbols (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: a system capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour when it is a system of physical symbols.
glossary.sentence: A physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: an example of a physical symbol system that uses symbols 0 and 1 to represent objects in the world.
glossary.sentence: The computer uses symbols 0 and 1. These symbols are combined into strings of 0 and 1 to represent objects in the world, e.g. the digit "3" is represented by the string "00110011". These strings are symbolic structures.

glossary.definition: a famous example of criticism against the physical symbol system hypothesis, described by John Searle, where a person in a room correctly manipulates Chinese symbols without understanding them.
glossary.sentence: John Searle’s Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism. Searle describes a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence.

glossary.definition: simplified models of the human brain consisting of a large number of connected processing units (artificial neurons) that mimic the neurons of the human brain.
glossary.sentence: This approach has emerged from attempts to mathematically model neural activity in the human brain and is based on so-called artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: in connectionist artificial intelligence, knowledge is not directly represented in the system but is considered as a distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units.
glossary.sentence: In connectionist artificial intelligence, there is an opposite view of the concept of knowledge: knowledge is not directly represented in the system because it is considered as a distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units, rather than a quantity encoded in physical symbols (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: processing units that mimic the neurons of the human brain and are connected to each other with numerical weights reflecting the strength of these connections.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks are simplified models of the human brain consisting of a large number of connected processing units (artificial neurons) that mimic the neurons of the human brain, and numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units, reflecting the strength of these connections.

glossary.definition: Numerical values assigned to factors in a heuristic evaluation function to reflect their relative importance.
glossary.sentence: We can add weights if we know that some factors are more important than others: f(n) = weight_1* the number of tiles out of place + weight_2*the sum of distances + weight_3*the number of tile reversals.

glossary.definition: the process of discovering relationships in data and reflecting them in the structure of connectionist models.
glossary.sentence: connectionist models recognise patterns in the data and reflect them in their structure (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: a program designed to play the board game Go that combines tree-based search with artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: for example, tree-based search complements artificial neural networks in the AlphaGo program, which is designed to play the board game Go (Goel, 2021).

glossary.definition: A representation of knowledge that proves to be learnable
glossary.sentence: Connectionist representation of knowledge proves to be learnable Symbolic representation of knowledge is not much learnable

glossary.definition: A representation of knowledge that is not much learnable
glossary.sentence: Connectionist representation of knowledge proves to be learnable Symbolic representation of knowledge is not much learnable

glossary.definition: An approach based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc.
glossary.sentence: Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc. ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . This is the only approach in which significant progress has been achieved so far.

glossary.definition: An approach that treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated. It aims to develop computer systems capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions.
glossary.sentence: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) . It is related to the development of such computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . Thus, this approach attempts to achieve a level of intelligent performance of an intelligent computer system where the system is able to solve a wide range of tasks, including novel tasks, and perform them as well as a human (Russell and Norvig, 2020) .

glossary.definition: An intelligence that surpasses human capabilities and is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills.
glossary.sentence: Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) .

glossary.definition: An approach that focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales. It asserts that true intelligence involves the ability to function in a real-world environment.
glossary.sentence: Additionally, to the previously mentioned approaches there is also the so called new/nouvelle artificial intelligence . “It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales” ( Floreano and Mattiussi, 2008). Practitioners of new artificial intelligence assert that true intelligence involves the ability to function in a real-world environment (Copeland, 2018; Pfeifer and Scheier, 2001) .

glossary.definition: A paradigm under new artificial intelligence that considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.
glossary.sentence: The main idea is that intelligence is a capability that arises when agents with simpler behaviours interact in the real environment and together achieve more complex behaviours (Copeland, 2018) . Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.

glossary.definition: A sub-direction under swarm intelligence that studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food.
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) . These two sub-directions are used in search and optimisation.

glossary.definition: A sub-direction under swarm intelligence that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish.
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) . These two sub-directions are used in search and optimisation.

glossary.definition: Artificial intelligence methods that do not use specific knowledge about a problem but rely on general approaches like search, logic, syntax-based computation, and automatic reasoning.
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: Artificial intelligence methods that incorporate a large amount of specific knowledge about the problem being addressed.
glossary.sentence: Incorporating knowledge into an intelligent computer system belongs to the so-called strong methods of artificial intelligence. Thus not all intelligent systems need the knowledge base because intelligent computer systems can also be developed using weak methods (symbolic manipulation or mathematical calculations).

glossary.definition: Systems developed to support humans in completing various functions and activities, or to cope with different types of problems.
glossary.sentence: At the beginning of the study course, you learnt out that intelligent computer systems are being developed to support humans in the completion of various functions and activities, or, in other words, to cope with different types of problems.

glossary.definition: A mathematical concept used to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.
glossary.sentence: The formal representation uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.

glossary.definition: A formal or mathematical representation, also called a formal specification, which is used to describe problem-solving processes formally.
glossary.sentence: We need a formal or mathematical representation, also called a formal specification (Figure 3).

glossary.term: initial state of the problem-solving process
glossary.definition: The current state in which we are at the beginning of solving a problem.
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process).

glossary.term: goal state of the problem-solving process
glossary.definition: The desired state we aim to reach when solving a problem.
glossary.sentence: a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process).

glossary.definition: The difference or gap between the initial state and the goal state, indicating that a problem exists.
glossary.sentence: whether there is a discrepancy between these two states (Figure 4).

glossary.definition: The sequence of steps or operations taken to resolve a problem, often guided by rules or logic.
glossary.sentence: ...intending to maximise the use of new information in the problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: The means and possibilities available to us for solving a problem, such as time, money, or transportation options.
glossary.sentence: Returning to our example of the job interview, you are more likely to evaluate the means of transport available to you, for example, you can use a bus, taxi, bicycle, or scooter. Additionally, you would evaluate how much time you could spend using each of these vehicles, what amount of money resources is available to you, and if there are currently traffic jams on the roads.

glossary.definition: Specific steps or operations that can be taken to transition from one state to another in a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: All of these aspects allow you to identify possible alternative actions, what kind of transport vehicles to use and under what conditions (Figure 6).

glossary.definition: Different options or strategies that can be chosen to solve a problem based on available resources and criteria.
glossary.sentence: Further, based on your subjective criteria, you would choose one alternative that best suits your specific circumstances.

glossary.definition: The representation of all possible game states and transitions in a game.
glossary.sentence: Although we have applied the minimax algorithm and alpha-beta pruning to a complete state space graph in this game, these principles can also be used in strategies for processing a partial state space graph.

glossary.definition: Points or elements in a state space graph that represent states, including search start nodes, goal nodes, and intermediate states.
The starting or topmost node in the game tree, having no ancestors.
glossary.sentence: However, there is no ancestor for the root node, and the algorithm therefore goes down again, via the first unconsidered path.

glossary.definition: A node in a state space graph that corresponds to a goal state and does not have descendants.
glossary.sentence: a state space graph should have at least one terminal (leaf) node, which corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that do not have descendants;

glossary.definition: Nodes in a state space graph that correspond to intermediate states and have both ancestors and descendants.
glossary.sentence: a state space graph may have internal nodes that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that have both ancestors and descendants.

glossary.definition: In a state space graph, arcs represent transitions between states or executable actions in a problem-solving process and should be directed to show the changing of states.
glossary.sentence: arcs that represent transitions between states or, in other words, executable actions in a problem-solving process. Arcs in the state space graph should be directed to show the changing of states in a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: A set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at a specified time, which changes when actions are performed.
glossary.sentence: A state is a set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time. Therefore, we can represent the problem-solving process as a structure showing states and transitions between them after performing actions (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: The movement from one state to another in a problem-solving process, achieved by performing specific actions.
glossary.sentence: This structure indicates that you need to pass through a series of intermediate states to get from an initial state of the problem-solving process to a goal state. The transition from one state to another state takes place by carrying out some action.

glossary.definition: A point or state in a graph or tree structure that represents a possible state of a problem.
glossary.sentence: the first and the third node will acquire a value of 5, while the second node will get a value of 3,

glossary.definition: A list that contains nodes that have been expanded or generated but are not among the best according to the beam width.

A list that contains the best nodes to be evaluated at each iteration, with its size determined by the beam width, excluding certain cases like iteration 0 or when the number of descendants/ancestors is less than the beam width.
The node being evaluated or processed by the algorithm at a given step.
glossary.sentence: When the algorithm has assigned an intermediate heuristic measure, it checks whether the ancestor of the current node in the current path also has an intermediate heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: The target or objective node in the state space graph that the algorithm aims to reach.
A search algorithm that limits the depth of its exploration to avoid going along indefinitely deep paths, but may miss solutions if the goal node is below the depth limit.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search overcomes a problem of going along indefinitely deep paths. At the same time, it is not able to find a solution path if the search goal node is located below the depth limit.

glossary.definition: A predefined maximum depth for the search algorithm to explore, which helps in avoiding infinite paths but may exclude valid solutions beyond this limit.
glossary.sentence: However, one of the main problems of Depth-Limited Search is a lack of guidelines on how to choose the depth limit for a particular problem.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that explores all nodes at the present depth level before moving on to nodes at the next depth level.
glossary.sentence: The difference between Breadth-First Search and Bidirectional Search is evident if we use the analogy of the state space graph and tree crown (Figure 21).

glossary.definition: A method of searching where nodes are explored in layers, starting from the root level and moving outward to subsequent levels.
glossary.sentence: Breadth-First Search explores the state space graph in a level-by-level fashion. This idea is represented in the title of the algorithm: “breadth” means to consider nodes sequentially at the same level before going deeper; “first” indicates finding the first path that leads to the search goal node and stopping the operation.

glossary.definition: A data structure that follows the ‘First in, First Out’ principle, where the first element added is the first one to be removed or processed.
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of this algorithm, new nodes are added to the right side of the OPEN list. Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a queue. A queue works according to the “First in, First Out” principle: the first data element entered is the first to be processed.

glossary.definition: A principle where the order of processing is based on the sequence of arrival, ensuring that the oldest element is processed before newer ones.
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of this algorithm, new nodes are added to the right side of the OPEN list. Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a queue. A queue works according to the “First in, First Out” principle: the first data element entered is the first to be processed.

glossary.definition: A thorough and complete search that examines all possible paths or states until the goal is found or all possibilities are exhausted.
glossary.sentence: Breadth-First Search is also guaranteed to find the search goal node if this node exists in the state space graph, as this algorithm performs an exhaustive search of the space state graph.

glossary.definition: The path with the least number of steps or transitions between states in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Bidirectional Search consumes less computational resources than Breadth-First Search. Moreover, it is also able to find the shortest path to the search goal node.

glossary.definition: The hardware and software capabilities required to execute an algorithm or process, often constrained by factors like memory and processing speed.
glossary.sentence: Unfortunately, Breadth-First Search has a serious shortcoming related to the consumption of computing resources. Russell and Norvig (2010) have provided time and memory estimates for Breadth-First Search processing a state space graph with an average of 10 descendants per node.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that performs a search simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm performs a search simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions.

glossary.definition: A set of rules or instructions that an intelligent computer system follows to process a graph and imitate game playing.
The available processing power, memory, or storage capacity for handling the state space graph.
A search approach where the system starts with known facts and infers conclusions from them.
glossary.sentence: the intelligent computer system can start by applying the data-driven search and demand the user to deliver known facts...

glossary.definition: A search approach where the system starts with a hypothetical solution and seeks facts to prove it.
glossary.sentence: ...then the system can switch on to the goal-driven search, as well as to switch back to the data-driven search when new facts are entering the system...

glossary.definition: A common node found in both OPEN lists during bidirectional search.
glossary.sentence: Bidirectional Search starts simultaneously from the search start node and the search goal node. It explores closer branches of both nodes until it finds a point of contact.

glossary.definition: The outermost part of a tree, analogous to the outer layers of a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Breadth-First Search explores closer branches and then goes to the ends of branches. Bidirectional Search starts simultaneously from the search start node and the search goal node.

glossary.definition: A single step or cycle in the algorithm's operation, where it processes and evaluates nodes.
Nodes that are generated by expanding a current node in the search process.
glossary.sentence: None of the generated nodes have been added to the OPEN or CLOSED lists. Thus, they all are evaluated by the heuristic evaluation function...

glossary.definition: Nodes that are predecessors in the search path.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm expands only one (the best) node located on the first position in the OPEN list. After generating and evaluating direct descendants/ ancestors...

glossary.definition: The initial node from which the search begins in the state space graph, such as node A in the example.
Experience-based rules that allow people to solve problems, make decisions, and reason in a more effective way.
glossary.sentence: Heuristics (or heuristic knowledge) are experience-based rules that allow people to solve problems, make decisions, and reason in a more effective way.

glossary.definition: Strategies used to find a path from the initial state to the goal state in an effective way.
glossary.sentence: Taking into account that a problem can have a large number of solutions, people use strategies (heuristic methods) to find a path from the initial state to the goal state in an effective way (Eysenck & Keane, 2000).

glossary.definition: A widely used concept that corresponds to heuristic knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Today, the widely used concept of “life hack” corresponds to what is understood by heuristic knowledge.

glossary.definition: A heuristic knowledge approach that does not guarantee a perfect outcome but usually leads to a good or acceptable outcome with less effort.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic knowledge is a rule of thumb: it will not give the perfect outcome, but it will nevertheless usually lead to a good or at least acceptable outcome with much less effort (Lieberman, 2012).

glossary.definition: A function that assigns heuristic measures to nodes based on their quality or proximity to the goal.
The process of searching through a state space graph to find a path from an initial state to a goal state.
glossary.sentence: In state space search, heuristic knowledge provides rules for choosing those paths in a state space graph that are most likely to lead to an acceptable problem solution (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A solution that is the best possible but may require significant resource consumption.
glossary.sentence: Ertel (2011) points out that, in practice, a good solution that is found more quickly is preferred over an optimal solution that costs a lot in terms of resource consumption.

glossary.definition: Algorithms that use heuristic knowledge to improve the effectiveness of finding the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: The effectiveness of finding the search goal node can be improved by using heuristic search algorithms.

glossary.definition: Search algorithms that cannot distinguish between paths that lead to the search goal node and those that do not.
glossary.sentence: Uninformed search algorithms cannot distinguish between paths that allow them to reach the search goal node and paths that do not.

glossary.definition: A specific criterion or measure used to evaluate nodes in a game or problem-solving scenario.
glossary.sentence: Applying the defined factor, the first and the third node will acquire a value of 5, while the second node will get a value of 3, and therefore, this could be considered a promising move.

glossary.definition: A move in a game or problem-solving scenario that is likely to lead to a favorable outcome based on certain evaluation criteria.
glossary.sentence: this could be considered a promising move. (The paragraph is based on the description given in (Luger, 2009))

glossary.definition: A measure that calculates the total distance of tiles from their intended positions, used to evaluate how close a state is to the goal.
glossary.sentence: Another factor that can be used to evaluate nodes is to sum up distances of tiles from their intended places (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: Pairs of tiles that are out of place and need to be swapped, often requiring multiple moves to correct.
glossary.sentence: We can define one more factor for this game: the number of pairs of tile reversals (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: Specific criteria or measures used in a heuristic evaluation function to assess states in a problem-solving scenario.
glossary.sentence: We can make combinations of these functions to create more complex heuristic evaluation functions, such as f(n) = the number of tiles out of place + the number of tile reversals.

glossary.definition: Individuals with specialized knowledge in a particular field who collaborate with developers to design effective heuristic evaluation functions.
glossary.sentence: The factors included in the function and their weights will be determined by the system’s developer in collaboration with domain experts.

glossary.definition: A technique used to validate and optimize heuristic evaluation functions based on data and patterns.
glossary.sentence: and will be validated using machine learning and optimization techniques.

glossary.definition: Methods used to improve the performance or effectiveness of a system, such as a heuristic evaluation function.
glossary.sentence: and will be validated using machine learning and optimization techniques.

glossary.definition: The resources required to calculate the values of a heuristic evaluation function, including time and computational power.
glossary.sentence: On the one hand, by including as many factors as possible in the function, the effectiveness of the search can be significantly improved—the number of visited nodes during the search decreases, and a path leading to the search goal node will be found more quickly.

glossary.definition: The combined expense of calculating heuristic measures and visiting nodes in a search algorithm.
glossary.sentence: This cost should be minimized. (The paragraph is based on the description given in (Luger, 2009))

glossary.definition: A precise answer to a problem that can be computationally intensive or infeasible due to resource limitations.
glossary.sentence: Let’s consider this case and relate it to the complexity of the state space graph. Heuristic knowledge is often used in the development of intelligent computer systems for problems that may have an exact solution, but there are not enough computational resources to find that solution.

glossary.definition: A limitation in a search algorithm where nodes beyond a certain depth cannot be seen or evaluated, often mitigated by using heuristic evaluation functions.
glossary.sentence: In this case, a so-called horizon effect occurs when the computer does not see nodes located beyond the specified depth, and therefore, it typically uses the heuristic evaluation function to be able to choose nodes that are most likely to lead to the search goal node.

glossary.definition: A scenario where only a portion of the state space graph can be explored due to limited computational resources, requiring the use of heuristic evaluation functions.
glossary.sentence: Only a part of a state space graph (a partly available state space graph). It is not possible to store and process the whole graph due to limited computational resources.

glossary.definition: A scenario where the entire state space graph can be stored and processed, often in problems with sufficient computational resources.
glossary.sentence: As a result, during the operation of an intelligent computer system, the state space graph is generated to the specified depth that is allowed by available computational resources. In this case, the use of the heuristic evaluation function is not always necessary.

glossary.definition: The average number of arcs or transitions per node in a state space graph, calculated as the sum of direct descendants divided by the total number of nodes.
glossary.sentence: In general, the complexity of a state space graph depends on two factors (Luger, 2009): B – the branching factor of the state space graph. It is the average number of arcs per node.

glossary.term: depth of the state space graph (L)
glossary.definition: The length of the longest path in a state space graph, representing the maximum distance from the root to any leaf node.
glossary.sentence: Usually, we acquire this factor by dividing the sum of direct descendants of all nodes with the total number of nodes in the state space graph. L – depth of the state space graph (usually the length of the longest path).

glossary.definition: The complete count of nodes present in a state space graph, which can be estimated based on branching factor and depth.
glossary.sentence: However, sometimes we do not know precisely the number of nodes in the graph and the depth of the graph. In this case, we need to analyze the problem to estimate the approximate average number of arcs per node and the length of the longest path.

glossary.definition: The relationship or dependency between variables, such as branching factor, depth, and total number of nodes in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The interdependence of the branching factor (B), the depth (L), and the total number of nodes (T) is presented by the plot that you can see in Figure 11.

glossary.definition: A heuristic search algorithm that always selects the best node based on a heuristic measure.
glossary.sentence: It should be noted that Hill Climbing always selects the best node. The best node is determined by the heuristic measures of nodes.

glossary.definition: Values assigned to nodes based on a heuristic evaluation function, where smaller values represent better quality or closer proximity to the goal.
A situation where the algorithm finds a node with a better heuristic measure than its descendants or ancestors, preventing it from reaching the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: The local maximum problem: the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors...

glossary.definition: A situation where the algorithm cannot continue the search because the heuristic measure of the best descendant or ancestor is equal to the current node's heuristic measure.
glossary.sentence: The plateau problem: the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm mentioned for comparison, which may not be as efficient as Beam Search in reaching the goal node.
A data structure used as the OPEN list in Best-First Search, where the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.
glossary.sentence: The OPEN list is implemented as a data structure called a priority queue (Figure 7). The priority queue works according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.

glossary.definition: A parameter that determines the maximum number of nodes kept in the OPEN list at each iteration.
An algorithm that expands nodes in the OPEN list simultaneously and selects the best nodes based on heuristic measures according to a specified beam width.
At the same time, without delay.
To generate descendants or ancestors of a node in the graph.
Nodes connected to a current node, depending on the search direction (forward or backward).
Terminal nodes in the graph with no descendants, representing goal states or end states.
To retreat or undo previous steps, which the algorithm does not do in case of failure.
A graph that includes all states from the initial to the goal states, typically used in simple games or when computational resources are sufficient.
A subset of the full state space, representing only explored or relevant states.
glossary.sentence: Although we have applied the minimax algorithm and alpha-beta pruning to a complete state space graph in this game, these principles can also be used in strategies for processing a partial state space graph.

glossary.definition: The starting point or root node of the game's state space graph.
Terminal nodes in the graph representing the desired outcomes or solutions.
A portion of a larger graph, such as a partial state space graph used when the full graph is unavailable.
Rules that define how states transition from one to another in the game.
A specified level or depth up to which the partial state space graph is generated.
An algorithm applied to a state space graph to process and evaluate states for game playing.
Nodes at the end of a sub-graph, representing states at a specified depth but not necessarily goal states.
The final outcome or score determined for a game node after evaluation.
glossary.sentence: The descendants of the current node have heuristic measures of 1 and −1. The current node is located at the MAX level, and thus acquires the maximum heuristic measure from its direct descendants. This is a game result.

glossary.definition: The node representing the present condition or position in the game during processing.
Individuals or entities involved in the game, labeled as maximiser and minimiser.
A player whose goal is to maximize their score or outcome in a game.
